name: chain-determinism-bench-v1
description: Multi-vendor chain-determinism observations from 22,000+ replayed agent runs across 11 LLMs. Per-run rows include prompt text (synthetic Sigma-registry queries) plus SHA-256 hashes for response, tool-call sequence, and final answer. Hash format is byte-identical to chain-receipt-sdk so each row can be replayed via `chain-receipt replay <hash>`. Released alongside the Chain-Divergence paper (Cruxia-AI, under peer review).
citeAs: @unpublished{ausili2026chaindivergence, title={Chain-Divergence: A Cross-Vendor Benchmark and Mechanism Probe for Replay-Attestable LLM Agents}, author={Ausili, Mars}, year={2026}, note={Under peer review. See https://chain-determinism.org/paper for current status.}}

distribution.name: phase_1a.jsonl
distribution.description: Phase 1a: T=0.0 multi-vendor replay (11 models x 40 held-out OOD queries x 50 runs = 22,000 rows).

distribution.name: phase_1b.jsonl
distribution.description: Phase 1b: T=0.5/1.0 temperature sweep (10 models x 40 queries x 20 runs x 2 temps = 8,000 rows; one model dropped from 1b run).

distribution.name: phase_2_v2.jsonl
distribution.description: Phase 2 v2: mechanism attribution on Qwen 2.5 72B Instruct (Modal vLLM A100-80GB:2). 1,600 hash-only Receipts: 40 held-out queries x 20 replays x 2 toggles (baseline_all_on, batch_invariant_proxy). baseline_all_on chain-divergence 27.5% [Wilson 95% CI 16.1%, 42.8%]; batch_invariant_proxy 0.0% [0.0%, 8.8%]; McNemar exact-binomial two-sided p = 0.000977.

distribution.name: phase_3_5_swebench.jsonl
distribution.description: Phase 3.5: SWE-bench Lite chain-divergence subset (3 models x 30 instances x ~2 runs = 164 rows).

recordSet.name: phase_2_v2
recordSet.description: Phase 2 v2 mechanism-attribution rows (Qwen 2.5 72B Instruct on Modal vLLM, two toggle conditions, 800 runs each, 1,600 total).

rai:dataCollection: Each row was generated by executing one agent run via direct vendor APIs: Anthropic Messages API (Claude Sonnet 4.5, Opus 4.6); OpenAI Chat Completions API (GPT-4.1, GPT-5.4, o3); OpenRouter (Llama 3.1/3.3 70B, Qwen 2.5 72B, DeepSeek V3.1, Mistral Large 2411, Gemini 2.5 Flash) with default routing; and Modal vLLM A100-80GB:2 (Phase 2 v2 mechanism cell on Qwen 2.5 72B Instruct). Phase 1a sampling: 11 vendors x 40 held-out OOD queries (Sigma-registry held-out generator, seed=2026, 8 query types x 5 surface variations) x 50 replays = 22,000 runs. Phase 1b: T=1.0 temperature sweep (10 vendors x 40 queries x 20 replays). Phase 2 v2: 40 queries x 20 replays x 2 toggles (baseline_all_on vs batch_invariant_proxy) = 1,600 runs. Phase 3.5: SWE-bench Lite chain-divergence subset (3 vendors x ~30 instances x ~2 replays = 164 rows). Concurrency=10 in Phase 1 batched mode; serial single-toggle runs in Phase 2 to ensure controlled-stack measurement. All runs at T=0 unless noted. Source code: scripts/paper5_multivendor_replay.py (Phase 1), scripts/paper5_mechanism_modal.py (Phase 2), scripts/paper5_swebench_lite_chain_div.py (Phase 3.5). Collection timeframe: March-April 2026.
rai:dataAnnotationProtocol: No human annotation was performed. Each row is a content-addressed agent-execution receipt; the only derived properties are computed mechanically from the raw run data: chain-divergence is a consistency property across multiple replays of the same query, computed by grouping rows on (vendor, model, query_id) and checking SHA-256 hash agreement of `tool_calls_hash` (canonical JSON serialization with sorted keys, NFC Unicode normalization). No annotators, labelers, or crowdworkers involved. Tool-call sequences are hashed via the same `compute_tool_calls_hash` helper used by the chain-receipt-sdk to ensure byte-identical hashes between dataset rows and SDK-emitted Receipts. Final-answer text is normalized to NFC Unicode and lowercased before hashing.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Positive impacts: (1) enables independent reproducibility audit of LLM agent systems by providing a public measurement protocol and reference dataset; (2) supports replay-based audit workflows that organizations may adopt under emerging regulatory frameworks (EU AI Act Articles 12 / 26 / 86, SR 11-7, HIPAA audit-control programs), with the explicit caveat that chain-determinism is a technical precondition rather than a satisfaction of any regulatory regime; (3) provides community measurement infrastructure that vendors can use to demonstrate or improve their own determinism profiles; (4) the calibrated-zero substrate methodology supplies a reusable instrument-design pattern for differential-measurement work in adjacent areas. Negative-impact / misuse considerations: (a) chain-divergence rates measured here are task-class-dependent and reflect a specific synthetic compositional benchmark; using them as a vendor reputation ranking would be a misuse and we explicitly disclaim that interpretation in the dataset documentation; (b) the metric does not capture semantic equivalence (commutative reorderings of independent tool calls are flagged as divergent), so headline rates may overstate user-visible non-determinism; (c) the dataset measures a system property rather than personal data and contains no harmful content, so direct misuse risk is low.
rai:dataReleaseMaintenancePlan: Maintained by the dataset creator; full plan in MAINTENANCE.md at the dataset root.
Verifier endpoint (chain-determinism.org/verify/{hash}) committed for at least 24 months from initial release on Cloudflare Workers + KV.
v0.1.0 will remain accessible on HuggingFace Datasets for at least 5 years; future versions are additive.

Semantic versioning: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH. MAJOR for backwards-incompatible Receipt schema changes; MINOR for new vendor support or additive Receipt fields; PATCH for bug fixes and documentation updates.
Each tagged release auto-archives to Zenodo with a stable DOI; placeholder 10.5281/zenodo.XXXXXXX assigned at submission, real DOI on first tagged release.
CHANGELOG.md follows the Keep a Changelog format and tracks all updates per repository.
Issue triage SLA: 14 days for the first 12 months post-publication.

Sunset policy: if maintenance becomes infeasible the maintainer will (1) post a deprecation notice at least 3 months in advance, (2) tag the final stable release, (3) archive all data and Receipts to a permanent academic archive (Internet Archive, Zenodo) with public access, (4) document an alternative static verifier path.
rai:dataUseCases: Chain-determinism measurement of new vendors: replicate Phase 1a methodology with chain-receipt-sdk and add vendor rows.
Cross-temperature reproducibility benchmarking: extend Phase 1b with additional temperature settings or vendors.
Mechanism-attribution research on serving-stack non-determinism: replicate Phase 2 v2 matched-pair toggle protocol on alternative serving stacks (TGI, SGLang, llama.cpp) or model architectures.
Audit-trail-protocol research using chain-receipt replay machinery and Receipt-level signature verification.

Calibration-instrument research using the bit-deterministic substrate (Lean theorems plus 499/499 differential test) as a deterministic-zero baseline for differential measurement of upstream variance.

OUT OF SCOPE: vendor reputation rankings (the metric is task-class-dependent and a low rate on this benchmark does not imply higher quality on other dimensions); regulatory compliance assessment (compliance is a legal determination requiring qualified counsel, not a technical measurement); adversarial robustness benchmarking (the metric rewards superficial template-locking and does not measure semantic determinism).
rai:dataBiases: Vendor selection bias: convenience sample of vendors accessible via Anthropic, OpenAI, and OpenRouter direct APIs at measurement time (March-April 2026). Excludes Gemini Pro, Bedrock-only proprietary models, Azure-only deployments, and on-premise models.

Sigma-registry contamination unverifiable: if a frontier vendor's pretraining corpus included material similar to the held-out OOD generator, chain-divergence rates may be artificially lower for that vendor.

OpenRouter routing variance: Phase 1a/1b rows for open-weights models (Llama 3.1/3.3, Qwen 2.5, DeepSeek V3.1, Mistral Large) routed via OpenRouter with default load-balancing across multiple backend serving providers. Provider routing was not pinned, so open-weights rates conflate model-level and serving-infrastructure-level non-determinism.

Reasoning-model temperature confound: o3 and gpt-5.4-turbo silently reject the temperature parameter at the API; their Phase 1a rows were requested at T=0 but the model actually ran at default ~T=1.0. The companion paper marks these explicitly and discusses the implication.

Vendor-format-incompatibility bias: Gemini 2.5 Flash and Mistral 7B v0.3 emit zero tool-call payloads under our OpenAI-compatible function-calling harness (a harness-side schema mismatch with each vendor's native tool-calling format, not a model-capability failure). They are excluded from cross-vendor comparison.
rai:dataLimitations: Effective sample size: the 40-query held-out subset is structured as 8 query templates x 5 surface variations; statistical analyses with effective sample size n_eff <= 8 on the query-template axis. Pair-level binomial CIs would be artificially narrow under within-query clustering and are not reported as inferential measures.

Vendor model versions are not pinned: OpenAI snapshot IDs and Anthropic API versions drift over time, so rates published may not reproduce on later snapshots. Each release pins a model-snapshot date in the README.

Byte-exact metric vs semantic equivalence: the metric does not capture semantic equivalence (commutative reorderings of independent tool calls are flagged as divergent). A 5-layer algorithmic normalizer absorbs only 1.0% of chain-divergent pairs on the full 158K-pair Phase 1a population (Appendix C of the companion paper); a 50-pair audit finds 0/50 trivial-equivalent (Wilson 95% upper bound about 7.1%, so the audit rules out large trivial-equivalence rates but not small ones).
Phase 1b rows lack latency and token-count fields (the original evaluation script did not capture them).

Phase 2 v2 raw rationales and final-answer text are retained locally with the dataset creator but not redistributed; only hashes and prompt text are released for that split. Vendor responses for Phase 1a/1b are similarly content-free (hash-only) by design.

Phase 3.5 SWE-bench Lite is a stub-environment preliminary: tools return content-keyed deterministic stubs, so observed chain-divergence is partly exploration-under-futility rather than real repair-plan variation. The companion paper Section 3.3 reports a real-Docker SWE-bench Substantive+ run (8,100 runs across 3 vendors) that supersedes this preliminary.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: No personal or sensitive information. Prompts are synthetic factual database-style questions about synthetic knowledge bases (e.g., 'find the busiest contributor on a synthetic biology corpus'); none reference real people, organizations, locations, or events. Vendor responses are not redistributed (only SHA-256 hashes are published). The dataset does not relate to people. No personally identifiable information (PII), no protected health information (PHI), no biometric data, no financial data.
